 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3ht","title":"Implement cloud CLI updates (vercel, wrangler, supabase, fly)","description":"## What
Implement the 'cloud' category for acfs-update:
- Vercel CLI: bun update -g vercel
- Wrangler (Cloudflare): bun update -g wrangler
- Supabase CLI: bun update -g supabase
- Fly CLI: Reinstall via official installer (flyctl self-upgrade)
- AWS CLI: Detect install method, update accordingly

## AWS CLI Handling (added)
AWS CLI can be installed multiple ways:
1. apt-installed: Handled by system updates (apt upgrade)
2. pip-installed: pip install --upgrade awscli
3. Official installer: Re-run installer or use aws --update

Detection: Check `which aws`, then `dpkg -s awscli` vs `pip show awscli`

## Technical Details
- Most cloud CLIs are bun global packages - simple updates
- Fly CLI has built-in: flyctl self-upgrade (or reinstall)
- Cloud CLIs may have authentication state - preserve it

## Considerations
- Only update tools that are installed
- Skip tools without prompting if not present
- For bun packages: bun update -g <pkg>
- Log version changes

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All cloud CLIs update correctly
- [ ] AWS CLI detected and updated properly
- [ ] Skips uninstalled CLIs
- [ ] Authentication preserved
- [ ] Version changes logged","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:43.995628-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:23.18063-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:23.18063-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in scripts/lib/update.sh:update_cloud() - Wrangler, Supabase, Vercel via bun install -g @latest","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3ht","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:25.514415-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3mp","title":"Implement runtime updates (bun, rust, uv, go)","description":"## What
Implement the 'runtimes' category for acfs-update:
- Bun: bun upgrade
- Rust: rustup update (stable, nightly if installed)
- uv: uv self update (or reinstall via official installer)
- Go: apt handles if system-installed, skip otherwise

## Technical Details
- Each runtime has different update mechanisms
- Bun: ~/.bun/bin/bun upgrade
- Rust: ~/.cargo/bin/rustup update
- uv: May need to source the installer again
- Go: Typically managed via apt, but could be manual install

## Considerations
- Must run as target user, not root (except go via apt)
- Capture before/after versions for each
- Skip if tool not installed (detected by missing binary)
- Rust may update multiple toolchains - capture all

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Each runtime updates correctly
- [ ] Skips uninstalled runtimes gracefully
- [ ] Version changes logged
- [ ] Works for both root and non-root users","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:07.808375-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:50.956617-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:13:50.956617-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented version tracking for bun/rust/uv, added update_go() function","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3mp","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:24.85834-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3nk","title":"Silence Next.js turbopack root warning in monorepo","description":"Next.js build warns about inferred Turbopack workspace root because there are multiple bun.lock files (repo root + apps/web). Configure apps/web/next.config.ts turbopack.root explicitly so Next/Turbopack uses the intended root and the warning goes away (no file deletions).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-20T15:30:19.912859-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T15:34:12.38145-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T15:34:12.38145-05:00","close_reason":"Set apps/web turbopack.root to the workspace root so Next/Turbopack resolves deps correctly and the monorepo root warning is silenced without deleting any lockfiles.","labels":["dx","web"]}
-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4ey","title":"Web Wizard Accounts Step (Pre-Install Guidance)","description":"## Purpose

Add a dedicated wizard step that guides users through account creation BEFORE they run the installer.
This ensures users have everything ready and reduces post-install friction.

## Why Before Install?

1. **Parallel work**: User creates accounts while installer runs
2. **No context switching**: Don't interrupt coding setup to create accounts
3. **Informed decisions**: User understands why each service matters
4. **Reduced abandonment**: Clear checklist = higher completion rate

## User Journey

```
[Provider Selection] → [VPS Setup] → [Accounts & Connections] → [Run Installer]
                                            ↑
                                      NEW STEP
```

## Step Design

### Header
"Set Up Your Accounts"
"These services will supercharge your vibe coding experience"

### Structure
1. **Required** (blocking - must have before proceeding)
   - None! All accounts can be created after. But we strongly encourage:
   
2. **Strongly Recommended** (do these first)
   - Google Account (if using SSO strategy)
   - Tailscale (for remote access)
   - Claude/Anthropic (primary coding agent)
   
3. **Recommended** (high value)
   - GitHub (code hosting)
   - Codex/ChatGPT Pro (secondary agent)
   - Vercel (deployment)
   
4. **Optional** (nice to have)
   - Supabase (database)
   - Cloudflare (CDN)
   - Gemini (third agent)

### Per-Service Card

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ [Logo] Tailscale                           [✓] Done     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Zero-config VPN for secure remote access                │
│                                                         │
│ Why: Access your VPS from anywhere without exposing     │
│      ports. No firewall config needed.                  │
│                                                         │
│ [Sign up with Google →]  [Other options ▾]              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Google SSO Callout

At the top of the step:
```
💡 Pro Tip: Use the same Google account for all services!
   
   One secure account = simpler logins + better security.
   Make sure your Google account has 2FA enabled.
   
   [Learn more about our security recommendations →]
```

### Progress Tracking

- Checkboxes for each service
- State saved to localStorage
- "Skip for now, I'll set these up later" button
- Summary shows: "3 of 8 accounts ready"

## Technical Implementation

### Service Catalog (lib/services.ts)

```typescript
interface Service {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  logo: string;  // SVG or image path
  category: 'access' | 'agent' | 'cloud' | 'devtools';
  priority: 'required' | 'recommended' | 'optional';
  shortDescription: string;
  whyNeeded: string;
  signupUrl: string;
  googleSsoUrl?: string;  // Direct link to Google SSO signup
  alternativeAuth?: string[];  // e.g., ["GitHub", "Email"]
  postInstallCommand?: string;  // e.g., "sudo tailscale up"
}
```

### AccountsStep Component

- Reads service catalog
- Groups by priority
- Renders ServiceCard for each
- Manages localStorage state
- Shows progress summary

### Navigation

- Can proceed without all accounts (not blocking)
- Show warning if skipping recommended services
- "Skip for now" vs "I've set these up" distinction

## Acceptance Criteria

1. New wizard step appears after VPS setup
2. All target services represented with accurate info
3. Google SSO links work correctly
4. Progress persists across page reloads
5. Mobile-responsive design
6. Accessible (keyboard navigation, screen readers)","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:59:04.244899-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:21:06.6346-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4ey","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4p7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:14.724796-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4jr","title":"Implement handle_checksum_mismatch() with skip/abort/proceed options","description":"# Task: Implement handle_checksum_mismatch() with batched prompts

## Purpose
When an upstream installer checksum does not match, provide user options while maintaining security.

## Critical UX Improvement: Batch All Mismatches

**Problem with per-tool prompts:**
If 5 tools have changed, user gets 5 separate prompts. Annoying and breaks flow.

**Solution:** Collect all mismatches first, then present ONE decision prompt.

```bash
declare -a CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES=()

# During verification phase (collect, dont prompt)
check_installer_checksum() {
    local tool="$1" url="$2" expected="$3"
    local actual=$(fetch_checksum "$url")
    
    if [[ "$actual" \!= "$expected" ]]; then
        CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES+=("$tool|$url|$expected|$actual")
        return 1  # Mark as mismatch but continue
    fi
    return 0
}

# After all tools checked, present batched decision
handle_all_checksum_mismatches() {
    if [[ ${#CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then
        return 0  # All good
    fi
    
    echo ""
    log_warn "Checksum mismatches detected for ${#CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]} installers:"
    echo ""
    for entry in "${CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]}"; do
        IFS="|" read -r tool url expected actual <<< "$entry"
        echo "  $tool:"
        echo "    Expected: ${expected:0:16}..."
        echo "    Actual:   ${actual:0:16}..."
    done
    echo ""
    echo "This usually means upstream scripts were updated."
    echo ""
    echo "Options:"
    echo "  [P] Proceed with new versions (update checksums.yaml later)"
    echo "  [S] Skip these tools, install everything else"
    echo "  [A] Abort installation"
    echo ""
    
    read -r -p "Choice [P/s/a]: " choice
    case "${choice,,}" in
        s) 
            for entry in "${CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES[@]}"; do
                SKIPPED_TOOLS+=("${entry%%|*}")
            done
            return 0
            ;;
        a) exit 1 ;;
        *) return 0 ;;  # Proceed (default)
    esac
}
```

## Non-Interactive Mode
In non-interactive (agent/CI) mode, behavior depends on tool classification:
- CRITICAL tool mismatch → abort (cant proceed safely)
- RECOMMENDED tool mismatch → auto-skip with warning

## Security Considerations
- Always log the actual vs expected hash
- Encourage users to verify upstream changes before proceeding
- Document how to update checksums.yaml

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tool classification (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-v8a)
- Used by: verify_and_run() flow (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-anq)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All mismatches collected before any prompt
- [ ] Single batched prompt for all mismatches
- [ ] Clear display of which tools affected
- [ ] P/S/A options with sensible defaults
- [ ] Non-interactive mode follows CRITICAL/RECOMMENDED rules","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:37.23646-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:46:15.314163-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:46:15.314163-05:00","close_reason":"Added checksum mismatch batching to security.sh: CHECKSUM_MISMATCHES array, record/clear/count/has functions, handle_all_checksum_mismatches() with P/S/A prompt, check_installer_checksum(), and _handle_mismatches_noninteractive() for CI/agent mode. Integrates with tools.sh for CRITICAL vs RECOMMENDED classification.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4jr","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-v8a","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:33.143559-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4ou","title":"EPIC: Automated Upstream Checksum Monitoring","description":"# Epic: Automated Upstream Checksum Monitoring

## Overview
Create a GitHub Action that daily monitors upstream installer scripts for changes and auto-creates PRs to update checksums.yaml.

## Problem Statement
Checksums get stale when upstream tools release updates. Users hit 'checksum mismatch' errors that are actually normal releases, not security issues. Maintainers don't check daily; users hit issues first.

## Business Impact
- Stale checksums = false security failures
- Automated PRs enable review (maintainer verifies legitimacy)
- Reduces user friction (fewer 'checksum mismatch' errors)
- Keeps project proactively maintained

## Technical Approach
1. Create .github/workflows/checksum-monitor.yml
2. Daily cron at 6am UTC + manual trigger
3. Run security.sh --verify --json to detect changes
4. If mismatches found, update checksums.yaml
5. Create PR with diff summary and review checklist
6. Optional: Slack/Discord notification for urgent updates

## Success Criteria
- PRs created within 24 hours of upstream changes
- PR body includes old/new checksums and verification checklist
- Maintainer can review and merge with confidence
- No user-facing checksum failures from stale data

## Files to Create/Modify
- .github/workflows/checksum-monitor.yml (new)
- scripts/lib/security.sh: Add --json output for --verify

## Reference
See: docs/ROADMAP_INSTALLER_RELIABILITY.md Section 6","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:41:27.836408-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:17:32.064598-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:17:32.064598-05:00","close_reason":"EPIC complete - all children implemented: GitHub Action workflow (coj), --json verify output (ss3), enhanced PR body (xos)"}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4p7","title":"EPIC: Accounts & Connections (Holistic Service Setup Experience)","description":"## Vision

Transform ACFS from "tool installer" to "complete environment setup" by adding comprehensive 
guidance for account creation, service authentication, and security best practices.

## Problem Statement

Users setting up a VPS for agentic coding face fragmented experiences:
1. **Tool installation** is handled by ACFS ✓
2. **Account creation** - No guidance (user figures it out)
3. **Authentication** - Ad-hoc, service by service
4. **Security posture** - Never explicitly addressed
5. **Connectivity** - Requires manual firewall/port setup

This friction causes:
- Abandoned setups ("I'll finish auth later" → never)
- Security gaps (weak passwords, no 2FA)
- Missed value (tools installed but not connected)
- Access headaches (can't reach VPS reliably)

## Solution: Three-Phase Approach

### Phase 1: Before Install (Web Wizard)
New wizard step: "Accounts & Connections"
- Explains which accounts to create and why
- Prioritizes: required → recommended → optional
- Recommends Google SSO for simplicity
- Provides signup links with SSO pre-selected
- Checklist tracks what user has set up

### Phase 2: During Install
- Tailscale installed automatically (apt repo method)
- Services added to manifest as modules
- Doctor checks verify connectivity

### Phase 3: After Install (Onboard TUI)
- Guided auth flow: "Run these commands to log in..."
- Progress tracking for each service
- Security checklist (2FA enabled? etc.)

## Key Services to Support

### Access Layer (P0 - Required)
- **Tailscale**: Zero-config mesh VPN for secure VPS access

### Coding Agents (P0 - Required)
- **Claude Code**: Primary AI coding agent (Google SSO via claude.ai)
- **Codex CLI**: OpenAI's agent (Google SSO via ChatGPT)
- **Gemini CLI**: Google's agent (native Google account)

### Cloud Platforms (P1 - Recommended)
- **Vercel**: Frontend deployment (Google SSO)
- **Supabase**: Database-as-a-service (Google SSO)
- **Cloudflare**: CDN/DNS/Workers (email-based)

### Developer Infrastructure (P1 - Recommended)
- **GitHub**: Code hosting (link Google email)

## Why Google SSO as Default Recommendation?

### Benefits
1. **Single identity**: One secure account protects all services
2. **Fewer passwords**: Less to remember, less to leak
3. **Strong baseline**: Google's security (2FA, suspicious login alerts) 
   automatically protects all linked services
4. **Robust recovery**: Google account recovery is well-established

### Risk Mitigation
- Single point of failure if Google compromised
- Mitigated by: 2FA + Advanced Protection Program recommendation
- Alternative flows documented for privacy-conscious users

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Web wizard has "Accounts & Connections" step
2. Tailscale installable via ACFS
3. Post-install guidance for service authentication
4. Doctor checks for Tailscale connectivity
5. Security recommendations documented

## Non-Goals (for this epic)

- Automatic account creation (requires OAuth flows)
- Credential storage/management (use native tools)
- Enterprise/team account handling","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:56:53.889307-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:27:51.302141-05:00"}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5m6.9","title":"Create clean canonical acfs.zshrc","description":"# Task: Create Clean Canonical acfs.zshrc

## Description
Create the production-ready zshrc that ACFS deploys to VPS.

## File: acfs/zsh/acfs.zshrc

## Requirements
Based on user's original zshrc but:
- Remove duplicate PATH/Bun blocks
- Remove duplicate aliases
- Use safe chpwd hook instead of overriding cd()
- Fix atuin path before init ordering
- Conditional tool aliases (only if tool exists)
- Support user overrides via ~/.zshrc.local

## Structure

```bash
# ~/.acfs/zsh/acfs.zshrc
# ACFS canonical zsh config (managed)

# --- SSH guard ---
# Prevent weird terminal settings over SSH

# --- Paths (early) ---
# ~/.local/bin, ~/.cargo/bin, go, bun, atuin

# --- Oh My Zsh ---
# Theme: powerlevel10k
# Plugins: git, autosuggestions, syntax-highlighting, etc.

# --- Editor preference ---
# vim over SSH, nvim locally

# --- Modern CLI aliases ---
# Only set if tool exists
# lsd/eza, bat, fd, rg, dust, btop, nvim, lazygit

# --- Git aliases ---
# gs, gd, gdc, gp, gpu, gco, gcm, gca, gb, glog

# --- Docker aliases ---
# dc, dps, dpsa, dimg, dex

# --- Directory shortcuts ---
# dev, proj, dots, p

# --- Custom functions ---
# mkcd, extract

# --- Safe ls after cd ---
# Using chpwd hook, not overriding cd()

# --- Tool settings ---
# UV_LINK_MODE, etc.

# --- Tool initializations ---
# cargo, atuin, zoxide, direnv, fzf

# --- Agent aliases ---
# cc, cod, gmi (dangerous mode enabled)

# --- Keybindings ---
# Ctrl/Alt+Arrow for word movement
# Home/End keys
# Force atuin Ctrl-R
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No duplicate code
- [ ] All tools alias only if present
- [ ] chpwd hook works correctly
- [ ] Atuin Ctrl-R works
- [ ] Local overrides supported
- [ ] Passes shellcheck","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:40.519614-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T11:59:34.896944-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T11:59:34.896944-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed shellcheck warnings, added directives for zsh-specific variables. File already met all requirements: no duplicates, conditional aliases, safe chpwd hook, proper atuin ordering, local overrides support.","labels":["config","task"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5m6.9","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5m6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-19T22:27:40.521482-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu","title":"EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence","description":"# Epic: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence

## Overview
Make install.sh truly resumable by writing state after each phase completes, not just at the end.

## Problem Statement
Currently, ~/.acfs/state.json is only written AFTER successful completion (install.sh ~line 1197-1210). If SSH disconnects at phase 8/10, users lose 15-20 minutes of work and must restart from scratch.

## Business Impact
- User Frustration: VPS SSH is unreliable; users close laptops, lose WiFi
- Completion Rate: Long installs (15-25 min) have high interruption probability
- Support Burden: 'I lost my progress' is a predictable complaint

## Success Criteria
- Re-running installer after disconnect resumes from last completed phase
- State file is atomic (no corruption on interrupt)
- Resume behavior is default; --force-reinstall overrides

## Files to Modify
- install.sh: Main state management logic
- scripts/lib/state.sh (new): Extracted state helpers

## Reference
See: docs/ROADMAP_INSTALLER_RELIABILITY.md Section 1","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:40:05.86043-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:03:45.447046-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:03:45.447046-05:00","close_reason":"All child tasks closed: 5zt (state schema), uxc (init/save), aa1 (atomic writes), d09 (corruption handling), 4xi (confirm_resume), yaj (run_phase wrapper), ywk (integration tests). Core phase-granular persistence implemented in scripts/lib/state.sh.","comments":[{"id":2,"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu","author":"jemanuel","text":"FYI: created `agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-mjt` (Manifest-Driven Installer Integration). This work will refactor `install.sh` to source generated installers + manifest_index and will touch selection semantics (`--only/--skip/--print-plan`) and curl|bash bootstrap (single archive).

Please keep this epic’s work compatible with your scope:
- orchestration/state/error-reporting/preflight remains install.sh + scripts/lib owned
- generated scripts stay source-safe (no top-level side effects)","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:50:07Z"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5vz","title":"Add installer security verification","description":"# Task: Add Installer Security Verification

## Description
Make the curl|bash pattern more trustworthy.

## Features

### 1. Checksum Verification
Before running sub-installers, verify checksums:
```bash
verify_checksum() {
    local url="$1"
    local expected_sha256="$2"
    local content=$(curl -fsSL "$url")
    local actual=$(echo "$content" | sha256sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
    if [[ "$actual" != "$expected_sha256" ]]; then
        log_error "Checksum mismatch for $url"
        exit 1
    fi
    echo "$content"
}
```

### 2. HTTPS Enforcement
All URLs must be HTTPS. Fail on HTTP.

### 3. --print Mode
Show exactly what will be downloaded:
```bash
install.sh --print
# Outputs:
# Will download:
# - https://bun.sh/install (sha256: abc123...)
# - https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh (sha256: def456...)
# ...
```

### 4. Source Links
Website shows link to install.sh source on GitHub.

### 5. Changelog
What changed since last version.

## Checksums File
```yaml
# checksums.yaml
installers:
  bun: "abc123..."
  uv: "def456..."
  # etc
```

-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5x9","title":"Tailscale Integration (Secure VPS Access)","description":"## Purpose

Add Tailscale to ACFS so users get zero-config secure VPS access out of the box.

## Why Tailscale?

### Traditional VPS Access Pain Points
- Must open SSH port (22) to public internet → attack surface
- Firewall rules to manage → complexity
- IP addresses to remember → friction
- Dynamic IPs require DDNS → more complexity
- Public IP exposure → privacy concern

### Tailscale Solves All of This
- **Private mesh network**: SSH never exposed to public internet
- **Zero firewall config**: Tailscale handles NAT traversal
- **MagicDNS**: Access by name (`ssh my-vps.tailnet.ts.net`)
- **Works anywhere**: Laptop, phone, another VPS
- **Google SSO**: One-click auth, consistent with our recommendation
- **Free tier**: 100 devices, 3 users - plenty for individual use

### Why Not Alternatives?
- **Raw WireGuard**: Requires manual key management, no discovery
- **ZeroTier**: Less polished UX, no Google SSO
- **Nebula**: More complex, designed for larger deployments
- **OpenVPN**: Heavy, complex, dated

Tailscale is the clear winner for our "vibe coding" audience.

## Implementation Approach

### Installation Method: APT Repository
Tailscale provides official apt repo. This is preferred over:
- Snap (sandboxing issues)
- Manual binary (no auto-updates)
- Docker (overkill for system service)

```bash
curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh
```

Or via apt directly:
```bash
curl -fsSL https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu/jammy.noarmor.gpg | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg] https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu jammy main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/tailscale.list
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y tailscale
```

### Post-Install Authentication
Tailscale requires interactive auth:
```bash
sudo tailscale up
```
Opens browser or prints auth URL. User logs in with Google.

### SSH Configuration (Optional Enhancement)
Configure SSH to use Tailscale IP:
```bash
tailscale ip -4  # Get Tailscale IP
# Add to ~/.ssh/config on client machine
```

Or use Tailscale SSH (beta):
```bash
sudo tailscale up --ssh
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `tailscale` module in acfs.manifest.yaml
2. Tailscale installed via apt repo method
3. Post-install message guides user to run `tailscale up`
4. `acfs doctor` checks Tailscale status
5. Works on Ubuntu 22.04, 24.04, 25.x

## Security Considerations

- Tailscale daemon runs as root (required for networking)
- Auth tokens stored in /var/lib/tailscale/
- Tailscale's security model is well-audited
- User should enable 2FA on Tailscale account","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:57:22.188553-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:57:22.188553-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5x9","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4p7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:14.536312-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zm","title":"Implement report_failure() with structured output","description":"# Task: Implement report_failure() with structured output

## Context
Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf)

## What to Do
Create a comprehensive failure report function:

### Human Output (terminal)
```
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║  ✖ INSTALLATION FAILED                                        ║
║                                                                ║
║  Phase 6/10: Language Runtimes                                 ║
║  Failed at: Installing Rust via rustup                         ║
║                                                                ║
║  Error: curl: (7) Failed to connect to sh.rustup.rs           ║
║                                                                ║
║  Suggested Fix:                                                ║
║    1. Check internet: curl -I https://google.com              ║
║    2. Retry: curl ... | bash -s -- --resume                   ║
║                                                                ║
║  Full log: /var/log/acfs/install.log                          ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
```

### JSON Output (log file)
Append structured JSON to install.log for tooling.

### With gum
Use gum style for prettier box drawing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Shows phase number and name
- Shows specific step that failed
- Shows error excerpt (truncated if long)
- Shows suggested fix from ERROR_PATTERNS
- Shows resume command
- Works with and without gum

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5zt","title":"Design state.json v2 schema","description":"# Task: Design state.json v2 schema

## Context
Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence

## Critical Design Decision: Track Phase IDs, Not Numbers

**Problem with tracking phase numbers:**
If we store `completed_phases: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` and later ACFS reorders phases or adds a new phase 3, the resume logic would skip the wrong phases.

**Solution:** Track phase IDs (stable identifiers):
```json
{
    "schema_version": 2,
    "completed_phases": ["user_setup", "apt_packages", "shell_setup", "cli_tools", "languages"],
    "current_phase": "agents",
    ...
}
```

## Full Schema

```json
{
    "schema_version": 2,
    "version": "0.1.0",
    "mode": "vibe",
    "started_at": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
    "last_updated": "2025-01-15T10:42:00Z",
    "completed_phases": ["user_setup", "apt_packages", "shell_setup"],
    "current_phase": "cli_tools",
    "current_step": "Installing ripgrep",
    "failed_phase": null,
    "failed_step": null,
    "skipped_tools": ["ntm", "bv"],
    "skipped_phases": ["postgres", "vault"],
    "phase_durations": {
        "user_setup": 12,
        "apt_packages": 45
    }
}
```

## Phase IDs (stable, never change meaning)
- user_setup
- apt_packages  
- shell_setup
- cli_tools
- languages
- tools
- database
- cloud
- agents
- stack
- finalize

## Backwards Compatibility
- If schema_version missing or 1: treat as legacy, offer fresh start
- Phase IDs are strings, won't conflict with old number-based format

## Acceptance Criteria
- Schema uses phase IDs not numbers
- Documented in code comments
- Backwards compatible with v1 detection
- All fields have sensible defaults","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:42:39.486596-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:43.137058-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:39:43.137058-05:00","close_reason":"Already implemented in scripts/lib/state.sh. Schema v2 uses phase IDs, is documented, and has backwards compatibility."}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7q1","title":"Wrap all phase functions with try_step calls","description":"# Task: Wrap all phase functions with try_step calls

## Context
Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf)

## What to Do
Update all install_phase_* functions to use try_step() for each operation:

### Before
```bash
install_phase_languages() {
    curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
    curl -fsSL https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
    # ...
}
```

### After
```bash
install_phase_languages() {
    CURRENT_PHASE_NAME="Language Runtimes"

    if ! command -v bun &>/dev/null; then
        try_step "Installing Bun runtime" \
            "acfs_curl https://bun.sh/install | bash" || return 1
    fi

    if ! command -v cargo &>/dev/null; then
        try_step "Installing Rust via rustup" \
            "acfs_curl https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y" || return 1
    fi
    # ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All phases use try_step for each operation
- Each step has descriptive name
- Existing command skip logic preserved
- return 1 on any step failure

## Files to Modify
- install.sh: All install_phase_* functions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:02.642601-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:02:12.698982-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:02:12.698982-05:00","close_reason":"Wrapped all 10 phase functions with set_phase() and try_step() calls. Total 98 try_step calls now in install.sh.  {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-85l","title":"Wizard: setCompletedSteps should notify same-tab listeners","description":"apps/web/lib/wizardSteps.ts setCompletedSteps writes localStorage but does not emit same-tab change events; pages like launch-onboarding call setCompletedSteps directly, so Stepper/useCompletedSteps can be stale until reload. Make setCompletedSteps emitStepsChange and avoid double-emission in markStepComplete.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T13:52:49.486053-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T13:53:19.718399-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T13:53:19.718399-05:00","close_reason":"Made setCompletedSteps emit same-tab change events so useCompletedSteps/Stepper update immediately; removed redundant emit in markStepComplete to avoid double notifications."}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-88b","title":"security.sh: avoid printing hash on fetch failure","description":"scripts/lib/security.sh fetch_checksum currently can emit the SHA of empty input when curl fails (because sha256sum still runs). Change it to only emit a hash when fetch succeeds (preserve exact bytes with sentinel).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"FuchsiaCreek","created_at":"2025-12-20T13:35:36.17546-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T13:36:44.037785-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T13:36:44.037785-05:00","close_reason":"Updated scripts/lib/security.sh fetch_checksum to only emit a checksum when curl fetch succeeds (preserves trailing newlines via sentinel), avoiding accidental empty-input hashes on network failure."}
-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8h4","title":"Onboard TUI Auth Flow Integration","description":"## Purpose

Add guided authentication flow to the `onboard` TUI that runs after ACFS installation.
This helps users authenticate all their services in a structured, step-by-step manner.

## Context

The onboard TUI (`packages/onboard/`) is an interactive tutorial that teaches users
Linux basics and the agent workflow. This epic extends it with an "auth" module that
guides users through authenticating each installed service.

## User Flow

After ACFS installs, user runs `onboard`:

```
$ onboard

Welcome to ACFS! What would you like to learn?

  [1] Linux Basics
  [2] Terminal Navigation  
  [3] Git Fundamentals
  [4] Authenticate Services  ← NEW
  [5] Your First Agent Session
```

Selecting "Authenticate Services":

```
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Service Authentication
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Let's connect your services. We'll go through each one.

  [ ] Tailscale     - Secure VPS access
  [✓] Claude Code   - Already authenticated!
  [ ] Codex CLI     - OpenAI coding agent
  [ ] Gemini CLI    - Google coding agent

Press Enter to start with Tailscale...
```

Then for each service:

```
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Tailscale Setup
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Tailscale gives you secure remote access to your VPS.
Once connected, you can SSH from anywhere!

Run this command:
  
  sudo tailscale up

This will open a browser (or show a URL).
Log in with your Google account.

[Press Enter when done, or 's' to skip]
```

After auth completes:

```
✓ Tailscale connected!
  Your Tailscale IP: 100.64.x.y
  
  Now you can SSH to this machine from any device
  with Tailscale installed:
  
  ssh user@your-vps.tailnet-name.ts.net

[Press Enter to continue to next service]
```

## Technical Implementation

### Auth Module Structure

```
packages/onboard/
├── src/
│   ├── lessons/
│   │   ├── auth/
│   │   │   ├── index.ts      # Auth lesson entry
│   │   │   ├── tailscale.ts  # Tailscale auth flow
│   │   │   ├── claude.ts     # Claude auth flow
│   │   │   ├── codex.ts      # Codex auth flow
│   │   │   └── gemini.ts     # Gemini auth flow
```

### Service Auth Check Functions

```typescript
// Check if service is authenticated
async function checkTailscaleAuth(): Promise<boolean> {
  const result = await exec('tailscale status --json');
  const status = JSON.parse(result.stdout);
  return status.BackendState === 'Running';
}

async function checkClaudeAuth(): Promise<boolean> {
  // Check for config file
  const configPath = path.join(os.homedir(), '.claude', 'config.json');
  return fs.existsSync(configPath);
}

// etc.
```

### Interactive Flow

Each service follows the pattern:
1. Check if already authenticated
2. If yes, show ✓ and skip
3. If no, explain service and show auth command
4. Wait for user to run command
5. Verify auth succeeded
6. Show success message and tips
7. Move to next service

## Dependencies

- Service catalog (same as web wizard)
- Auth check functions for each service
- Terminal UI framework (blessed, ink, or similar)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `onboard` has "Authenticate Services" option
2. Each installed service has an auth flow
3. Auth status is checked before and after
4. Clear instructions and visual feedback
5. User can skip individual services
6. Progress is tracked (which services authed)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:01:46.476515-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:01:46.476515-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8h4","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4p7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:14.901839-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8mv","title":"Add --strict flag for legacy checksum behavior","description":"# Task: Add --strict flag for legacy checksum behavior

## Context
Part of EPIC: Checkpoint-Based Checksum Recovery (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-tx7)

## What to Do
Add --strict flag that restores the original behavior where ANY checksum mismatch aborts installation.

### Behavior
- Without --strict (default): Use new recovery flow
- With --strict: All tools treated as critical, any mismatch aborts

### Use Cases
- Security-conscious users who want no exceptions
- CI/CD environments where reproducibility matters
- Auditing/compliance scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
- --strict flag parsed in argument handling
- When set, all tools treated as critical
- Help text documents the flag
- Default behavior is recovery flow

## Files to Modify

Comprehensive testing of acfs-update:
1. Test on fresh ACFS install (all tools present)
2. Test on partial install (some tools missing)
3. Test each category individually
4. Test --dry-run mode
5. Test --yes mode (non-interactive)
6. Test failure handling (simulate failures)
7. Test logging output

## Test Scenarios
- Fresh VPS with full ACFS install
- Existing VPS with previous ACFS version
- Missing tools (should skip gracefully)
- Network failures (should report and continue)
- Permission issues (should report and continue)

## Considerations
- Need a test VPS or Docker container
- Some tests may need mocking (network failures)
- Should test as both root and non-root user

- [ ] All categories update correctly
- [ ] Dry-run shows accurate preview
- [ ] Failures handled gracefully
- [ ] Logs are comprehensive and useful
